posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.cooked:
So you buy 3 bottles of a wine (which is of course is the 1996 Haut Marbuzet in this case). You pay something like US$20 for it, you cellar it for 23 years and then magic happens. Thank you to Ch. Haut Marbuzet for making such a lovely delicious wine for such a great price.

This is the first bottle I have tried and wow what a wonderful “little” wine from a Cru Bourgeois. Man this is drinking well. Cork was pristine and came out easily. This was a pop and pour as it was a random Wednesday night choice.

This is such a pleasure to drink, lovely crisp fresh and very alive. Cassis and cigar box on the nose, beautifully judged ripe cassis fruits with lovely acidity and fine grained tannins. This is not the most powerful, intense or complex Bdx I have had, but in terms of pleasure in the glass for the price, it is a real winner. The bottle says 12.5% which I think I believe based on the fruit profile and the crisp taste.

Thank you Ch Haut Marbuzet for making such a wine that has truly rewarded time in the cellar and it delivering a lot of drinking satisfaction. Oh the joys of mature Bdx from wines that have been in the cellar since release.

Yum

Brodie

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked: Thanks for the note. US distribution was spotty in the past for this estate, as they sell much directly to private clients.

posts.name: Todd Hamina
posts.cooked: This is what wine is all about. Magic.

posts.name: Ashley Bair
posts.cooked: Thank you for the note, Brodie. I greatly enjoyed a bottle of the 1996 Haut-Marbuzet a few years ago, and am happy, but not surprised, to hear that it is still holding up so nicely.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
They used to have mature bottles of this at Chez Jenny in Paris.
A friend sold them the bulk of their barrels so I can always go to his house.

posts.name: Todd Tucker
posts.cooked:
Thanks Brodie for the fine note and better commentary. One of my greatest joys in wine is drinking a mature humble wine punching above its weight. The experience may be less jaw dropping than a supposedly special bottle, but the happiness is usually greater. Thanks for sharing your note. Hope you and your family are well.

Best,

Todd

posts.name: Jen M a s o n
posts.cooked: This is the producer that turned me into a wine lover. Twenty something and on one of my first business trips to paris in the early 90’s. I dont know the vintage we drank but i hunted this wine ever since. Seems greatly under apprecaited for what it is.
posts.display_username: Jen M a s o n

posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.cooked:
Thank you for the note, Brodie. I greatly enjoyed a bottle of the 1996 Haut-Marbuzet a few years ago, and am happy, but not surprised, to hear that it is still holding up so nicely.

I just hope my remaining 2 bottles each of the 1988 and the 1982 drink as well (even close would be great)

cheers Brodie

posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.cooked:
One of my greatest joys in wine is drinking a mature humble wine punching above its weight. The experience may be less jaw dropping than a supposedly special bottle, but the happiness is usually greater.

Hi Todd, all good in NZ.

Isn’t this so true. Clearly there is psychological dimension in the manner in which we both appreciate mature wines. I remember years ago Francois Audouze talking about (and making up a rating system) about rating wines versus expectations. With these less famous wines, our expectation threshold is lot lower and thus the satisfaction levels tend to be higher. Plus everyone loves a bargain.

All the best to you and Kim

Brodie

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
This is what wine is all about. Magic.

Exactly.

And the magic feels so much better when it is coming from a value-play than a unicorn. This is the holy grail of daily drinkers, no? I’m enjoying a 2012 Sociando Mallet right now, so much so that I am likely pulling the trigger on a new case. I searched after popping this last night, finishing it now. I originally bought six. Right now this is my 96 Haut Marbuzet, just younger.

Love this thread.

posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.cooked:
Day 2 update: The unfinished half bottle was gassed with Argon and put back in the cellar. Basically the wine has hardly budged an inch, tastes just as delicious as it did yesterday. Nose is nicely fragrant but with perhaps a hint of dried leaf showing up. Plate presence unchanged and the finish is really good and long. A real keeper - too bad there are only two more in the cellar. Nice problem to have

thanks for all the comments

brodie
